label: Imperfect (imperfetto)
description: The 'background' past tense. Formed by adding -av-/-ev-/-iv- + the imperfect endings to the verb stem. Used for habitual, ongoing, or descriptive past actions, contrasting with the passato prossimo's completed-event reading. Has a small irregular core (essere, plus a stem-expansion class with fare, dire, bere, porre, tradurre). Modal verbs (sapere, dovere, potere, volere, conoscere) shift meaning between imperfect and passato prossimo: the imperfect is the state, the passato is the event.

label: Formation
description: Imperfect formation is the most regular of Italian's past tenses. Stem (drop infinitive ending) + tense-class marker (-av- for -are, -ev- for -ere, -iv- for -ire) + the universal endings (-o, -i, -a, -amo, -ate, -ano). Only a small core of verbs is irregular: essere has a wholly distinct form (ero), and a stem-expansion class (fare, dire, bere, porre, tradurre) restores a longer Latin stem before the regular endings (facevo, dicevo, bevevo, ponevo, traducevo).

label: Regular -are: parlavo / parlavi / parlava / parlavamo / parlavate / parlavano
description: Stem + -av- + universal endings. Stress falls on the -av- in the singular and 3pl forms (parlAvo, parlAva, parlAvano) and shifts to the ending in 1pl/2pl (parlavAmo, parlavAte). The largest and most predictable class.

attributes.common_miss: stress misplacement on 1pl/2pl

label: Regular -ere: vedevo / vedevi / vedeva / vedevamo / vedevate / vedevano
description: Stem + -ev- + universal endings. Includes -ere verbs that are irregular elsewhere but regular in the imperfect (prendere becomes prendevo, scrivere becomes scrivevo, leggere becomes leggevo). The regularity of -ere in the imperfect is a feature worth flagging to learners surprised that prendere has no irregular imperfect.

attributes.common_miss: assuming the participle-irregularity carries over (preso giving presevo) instead of using the regular stem

label: Regular -ire: dormivo / dormivi / dormiva / dormivamo / dormivate / dormivano
description: Stem + -iv- + universal endings. The -isc- pattern (capisco-style) does NOT apply to the imperfect: capire is capivo, not capiscevo. This is a high-frequency miss for learners who have internalised -isc- for the present tense.

attributes.common_miss: carrying -isc- into the imperfect (capiscevo for capivo)

label: Irregular forms
description: Italian's imperfect has a remarkably small irregular set. Essere is wholly distinct (ero, eri, era, eravamo, eravate, erano). A stem-expansion class (fare, dire, bere, porre, tradurre and similar) reverts to the longer Latin infinitive stem before adding the regular -ev- and endings.

label: Essere: ero / eri / era / eravamo / eravate / erano
description: The most irregular imperfect in Italian. The stem ER- is wholly different from the present's es-/si-/è- forms. Worth memorising as a unit. High-frequency.

attributes.common_miss: regular-ising to 'essevo' or carrying 'sono/sei' patterns over

label: Stem-expansion verbs (fare, dire, bere, porre, tradurre)
description: A small class of high-frequency verbs whose contracted modern infinitive opens out into the longer Latin stem before the imperfect endings. Fare uses fac-, dire uses dic-, bere uses bev-, porre uses pon-, tradurre uses traduc-. The same stem-expansion drives their past-participle irregularities (fatto, detto, bevuto, posto, tradotto).

label: Fare: facevo / facevi / faceva / facevamo / facevate / facevano
description: From the Latin stem fac-. Among the highest-frequency irregular imperfects. Maps to the imperfect participle pattern (also fac-: fatto in the participle).

attributes.canonical_example: facevo i compiti, facevamo una passeggiata
attributes.common_miss: using a regular stem (favo, faevo)

label: Dire: dicevo / dicevi / diceva / dicevamo / dicevate / dicevano
description: From the Latin stem dic-. Common in indirect speech ('ha detto che diceva...'). Same stem appears in the participle (detto).

attributes.canonical_example: diceva sempre la verità, dicevamo che...
attributes.common_miss: regular-ising to divo/dievo

label: Bere: bevevo / bevevi / beveva / bevevamo / bevevate / bevevano
description: From the Latin stem bev-. Same stem in the participle (bevuto) and across most other tenses.

attributes.common_miss: regular-ising to bevvo

label: Porre and -porre compounds: ponevo / componevo / opponevo / supponevo / proponevo
description: From the Latin stem pon-. Lower-frequency than fare/dire/bere but the same pattern. Compounds (comporre, opporre, supporre, proporre) all follow.

attributes.common_miss: regular-ising to porre giving porevo

label: Tradurre and -durre compounds: traducevo / producevo / introducevo / riducevo
description: From the Latin stem traduc-/duc-. Compounds (produrre, introdurre, ridurre, sedurre, condurre) all follow.

attributes.common_miss: regular-ising to tradurrevo or traducovo

label: Usage (what imperfect is for)
description: Imperfect describes a past action without specifying its completion. Habitual ('I used to walk to school'), ongoing ('it was raining'), descriptive ('she was tall'), backgrounded relative to a foregrounded passato-prossimo event. Also has specific lexicalised uses: telling time and age in the past, weather descriptions, and a polite-request use ('volevo un caffè').

label: Habitual / repeated past ('used to', 'would')
description: Imperfect is the default for habitual past actions. Maps to English 'used to' and habitual 'would' ('When I lived in Rome, I would walk to the office every day'). Cued by adverbs like sempre, di solito, ogni giorno, da bambino.

attributes.canonical_example: Da bambino giocavo a calcio ogni domenica
attributes.common_miss: using passato prossimo for habituals (ho giocato instead of giocavo)

label: Ongoing or descriptive past ('was doing', 'was')
description: Imperfect describes an in-progress action or a state without endpoints. Maps to English past progressive ('was walking') and to stative descriptions ('he was tired', 'the house was big'). The aspectual contrast with passato prossimo is sharpest here: a state isn't an event.

attributes.canonical_example: Pioveva forte e la strada era deserta
attributes.common_miss: using passato prossimo for states (è stata stanca for the state, instead of era stanca)

label: Background with a foregrounded passato prossimo event
description: Imperfect sets the scene; passato prossimo introduces the event that interrupts or punctuates it. 'Mentre studiavo, è suonato il telefono' (while I was studying, the phone rang). The two tenses share the sentence: imperfect for the ongoing context, PP for the discrete happening.

attributes.canonical_example: Mentre dormivo, qualcuno ha bussato alla porta
attributes.common_miss: swapping the tenses (ho dormito mentre bussava)

label: Duration in the past (da + time + imperfect)
description: To say how long a past action had been going on, Italian uses imperfect + da + time-phrase: 'Vivevo a Roma da tre anni quando ho incontrato Maria' (I had been living in Rome for three years when I met Maria). English maps to past perfect continuous; Italian uses imperfect + da.

attributes.canonical_example: Aspettavo da venti minuti quando è arrivata
attributes.common_miss: using PP (ho aspettato da venti minuti) instead of imperfect

label: Age, time of day, and weather in the past
description: Fixed-pattern uses: avevo X anni (I was X years old), erano le tre (it was three o'clock), faceva freddo / pioveva / nevicava (the weather was X). All take imperfect, never PP, because they describe states rather than events.

attributes.common_miss: using PP (ho avuto dieci anni, ha fatto freddo) for these states

label: Polite imperfect (softening a request)
description: Italian uses the imperfect (especially of volere) to soften a present request. 'Volevo un caffè' (I'd like a coffee) is more polite than 'voglio un caffè'. Strictly speaking it's a present meaning expressed in the past tense; the politeness comes from the distancing effect.

attributes.common_miss: missing the politeness reading and over-translating as 'I wanted'

label: Imperfect vs passato prossimo (the key discrimination)
description: The main difficulty for learners. Imperfect for habits, states, and background; passato prossimo for completed events. Cued by adverbs (ieri = PP, sempre/di solito = imperfect; in genere = imperfect, l'altro giorno = PP). The modal verbs are a special case: imperfect and PP differ in meaning rather than just aspect.

label: Choosing imperfect or PP (general aspect rule)
description: General-aspect discrimination not tied to a specific modal. Imperfect for ongoing or repeated, PP for single completed event. Adverb cues are reliable: sempre, di solito, ogni X, mentre, da X for imperfect; ieri, l'altro giorno, una volta, all'improvviso, una settimana fa for PP.

canonical_examples: Andavo a scuola in autobus (habitual) vs Ieri sono andato a scuola in autobus (single event)
attributes.common_miss: defaulting to PP for English past-tense verbs regardless of aspect

label: Modal verbs: imperfect vs PP shifts meaning
description: With sapere, dovere, potere, volere, conoscere, the imperfect and PP convey different meanings, not just different aspects. The imperfect is the state or disposition; the PP is the resulting event. This is one of the most diagnostically rich areas of Italian grammar and a sleeper for English-speaking learners.

label: Sapere: sapevo (I knew, state) vs ho saputo (I found out, event)
description: The cleanest case. Sapevo che era malato = I knew he was ill (state of knowledge). Ho saputo che era malato = I found out he was ill (the moment of learning). English glosses the two as 'knew' vs 'found out', not as 'knew' vs 'have known'.

canonical_examples: Sapevo che veniva (I knew he was coming)
Ho saputo che veniva (I found out he was coming)
attributes.common_miss: using imperfect for the event-of-learning reading

label: Dovere: dovevo (had to, was supposed to) vs ho dovuto (had to, and did)
description: Dovevo andare = I was supposed to go (open whether I went). Ho dovuto andare = I had to go (and I did). The imperfect leaves the action's completion ambiguous or unstated; the PP confirms it happened. Sentences with explicit follow-through usually take PP.

canonical_examples: Dovevo studiare ma sono uscito (was supposed to, didn't)
Ho dovuto studiare (had to, and did)
attributes.common_miss: interchanging the two and missing the completion implication

label: Potere: potevo (could, was able to) vs ho potuto (was able to, and did)
description: Potevo andare = I could have gone / was able to go (didn't necessarily). Ho potuto andare = I was able to go (and I did). The PP form often carries an 'against odds' or 'finally managed' nuance ('ho potuto vederlo' = I managed to see him).

canonical_examples: Potevo aiutarti (I could have helped you)
Ho potuto aiutarti (I managed to help you)
attributes.common_miss: defaulting to imperfect for both because English 'could' is ambiguous

label: Volere: volevo (wanted to) vs ho voluto (insisted on, got my way)
description: Volevo parlare = I wanted to speak (open whether I did). Ho voluto parlare = I insisted on speaking (and did). The PP form often carries a strong-will or insistence nuance.

canonical_examples: Volevo dirti una cosa (I wanted to tell you something)
Ho voluto dirgli la verità (I insisted on telling him the truth)
attributes.common_miss: missing the insistence reading in the PP form

label: Conoscere: conoscevo (knew, was acquainted) vs ho conosciuto (met for the first time)
description: Conoscevo Maria = I knew Maria (was acquainted). Ho conosciuto Maria = I met Maria (the event of being introduced). This is one of the most pedagogically vivid imperfect/PP contrasts because it changes the verb's English equivalent entirely.

canonical_examples: Conoscevo già Maria (I already knew Maria)
Ho conosciuto Maria alla festa (I met Maria at the party)
attributes.common_miss: translating PP as 'have known' instead of 'met'

label: Imperfect in indirect speech
description: When the reporting verb is in a past tense, present-tense speech shifts to imperfect: 'Ha detto: vengo' becomes 'Ha detto che veniva'. English does the same ('he said he was coming'). High-frequency across reporting, narrative, and conversation.

label: Reported present-tense speech becomes imperfect
description: Direct: 'Sono stanco'. Indirect (with past reporting verb): 'Ha detto che era stanco'. The present 'sono stanco' shifts to imperfect 'era stanco'. The same shift applies to direct-speech progressives ('sto lavorando' becomes 'stava lavorando').

attributes.canonical_example: Mi ha detto che era stanco
attributes.common_miss: leaving the embedded clause in the present (ha detto che è stanco)

label: Reported intention or future plan becomes imperfect (or imperfect of stare + per)
description: Direct: 'Vado al cinema'. Indirect: 'Ha detto che andava al cinema'. Direct: 'Sto per uscire'. Indirect: 'Ha detto che stava per uscire'. The imperfect carries the prospective reading of a past-tense report.

attributes.canonical_example: Ha detto che andava al cinema
attributes.common_miss: using future tense in the embedded clause (ha detto che andrà)

label: Stylistic and register uses
description: Higher-register or marked uses of the imperfect: the narrative imperfect (used for vivid storytelling, often as a stand-in for the passato remoto), and the colloquial counterfactual ('se avevi tempo, venivi' as an informal substitute for 'se avessi avuto tempo, saresti venuto').

label: Narrative imperfect (vivid storytelling)
description: Imperfect used in literary or journalistic narration to recount discrete past events, lending an immersive 'in the moment' feel. 'Il treno partiva alle otto di sera; alle dieci arrivava a Milano.' Functionally substitutes for PP or passato remoto. Common in older fiction and in headlines.

attributes.canonical_example: Il giorno dopo partiva per Roma
attributes.common_miss: reading the imperfect as habitual when it's narrating a single event

label: Colloquial counterfactual (substitute for subjunctive + conditional)
description: In informal Italian, the imperfect can replace both the imperfect-subjunctive protasis and the conditional apodosis of a hypothetical: 'Se avevi tempo, venivi con noi' instead of 'Se avessi avuto tempo, saresti venuto con noi'. Widespread in speech, prescriptively dispreferred in writing.

attributes.common_miss: translating literally and missing the counterfactual reading
